bytes.topic: Contract Architecture — upgradeable patterns (proxy), libraries, interfaces, abstract contracts
bytes.title: Vyper Smart Contract Upgrade Patterns with Proxy Contracts
bytes.body: Vyper's proxy contract pattern enables seamless upgrades to smart contracts without affecting their storage. This is achieved by separating the logic and storage into two separate contracts. The proxy contract acts as an entry point, delegating calls to the logic contract. To implement this pattern, define an interface for the logic contract using the @external decorator, then create a proxy contract that inherits from the interface. The proxy contract's constructor initializes the logic contract address. When upgrading the logic contract, simply deploy a new logic contract and update the proxy contract's logic contract address.

bytes.topic: Solidity Fundamentals — types, visibility, state variables, functions, modifiers
bytes.title: Vyper Fundamentals: Mastering Types, Visibility, and State Variables
bytes.body: Vyper, a Python-based smart contract language, provides a unique set of features for building robust and secure blockchain applications. When defining state variables, Vyper supports various types such as uint256, address, and bool. Visibility modifiers like public, private, and external can be used to control access to functions and state variables. For instance, the @external decorator can be used to expose functions to external contracts. Understanding these fundamentals is crucial for writing efficient and secure Vyper contracts.
bytes.code_snippet:
a: uint256 = 5
b: address = 0x1234567890
@external
def greet() -> str: return 'Hello World!'

bytes.topic: Storage Layout — slots, packing, SLOAD/SSTORE cost, avoiding storage collisions
bytes.title: Vyper Storage Layout — Optimizing SLOAD/SSTORE Costs
bytes.body:
When storing data in Vyper smart contracts, it's essential to consider the storage layout to minimize SLOAD/SSTORE costs. Slots are 32-byte positions in the storage trie where contract variables are stored. To pack multiple variables into a single slot, use the `calldata` type, which allows for flexible packing. For example:
```
variable1: bytes32 = "0x1234567890abcdef" # 32 bytes
variable2: uint256 = 1234567890
# variable2 is packed into the last 32 bits of variable1's slot
```
To avoid storage collisions, use the `storage_layout` decorator to define the storage layout for your contract. This ensures that variables are stored in the correct slots.
```
@storage_layout
class MyContract: # storage_layout decorator defines the storage layout
    variable1: bytes32 = "0x1234567890abcdef"
    variable2: uint256 = 1234567890
```
By optimizing the storage layout, you can reduce SLOAD/SSTORE costs and improve contract performance.
bytes.code_snippet:
```
variable1: bytes32 = "0x1234567890abcdef"
variable2: uint256 = 1234567890
```
@storage_layout
class MyContract:
    variable1: bytes32 = "0x1234567890abcdef"
    variable2: uint256 = 1234567890
